instruction: Implement a custom iterator in Rust that generates a Fibonacci-like sequence with a configurable window size (e.g., tribonacci sums last 3 elements). The iterator should support both bounded and unbounded generation, and demonstrate composing it with standard iterator adaptors.
output:
<think>
To build a custom iterator in Rust, I need to implement the Iterator trait with its required `next()` method. For a generalized Fibonacci with window size N, I need to maintain a VecDeque of the last N values and sum them to produce the next value.

The struct needs: the window of previous values, a max count (Option<usize> for bounded/unbounded), and a counter. The Iterator trait requires specifying the Item type — here u128 to handle large values before overflow.

For the window: VecDeque is perfect since I need to push_back and pop_front efficiently. Each call to next() sums the window, pushes the sum, pops the oldest if window exceeds size, and increments the counter.

Initialization matters: for standard Fibonacci, we start with [0, 1]. For tribonacci, [0, 0, 1]. I'll provide a `new()` that takes a seed slice and window size, validating that the seed length equals the window size.

For composability with iterator adaptors, I just need Iterator implemented — then .take(), .filter(), .map(), .zip(), .collect() all work automatically. I'll demonstrate chaining several adaptors.

Bounded vs unbounded: if max_count is None, the iterator runs forever (caller must use .take() or break). If Some(n), it yields exactly n items then returns None. This is the standard pattern for Rust iterators.

I should also implement size_hint() for bounded iterators, which helps collect() pre-allocate.
</think>

```rust
use std::collections::VecDeque;

struct WindowFib {
    window: VecDeque<u128>,
    window_size: usize,
    max_count: Option<usize>,
    yielded: usize,
    started: bool,
}

impl WindowFib {
    fn new(seeds: &[u128], max_count: Option<usize>) -> Self {
        assert!(!seeds.is_empty(), "Need at least one seed value");
        let window_size = seeds.len();
        let window: VecDeque<u128> = seeds.iter().copied().collect();
        WindowFib {
            window,
            window_size,
            max_count,
            yielded: 0,
            started: false,
        }
    }

    fn fibonacci(max_count: Option<usize>) -> Self {
        Self::new(&[0, 1], max_count)
    }

    fn tribonacci(max_count: Option<usize>) -> Self {
        Self::new(&[0, 0, 1], max_count)
    }
}

impl Iterator for WindowFib {
    type Item = u128;

    fn next(&mut self) -> Option<u128> {
        if let Some(max) = self.max_count {
            if self.yielded >= max {
                return None;
            }
        }

        self.yielded += 1;

        // First, yield elements from the seed window
        if !self.started {
            if self.yielded <= self.window_size {
                return Some(self.window[self.yielded - 1]);
            }
            self.started = true;
        }

        // Compute next as sum of window
        let next_val: u128 = self.window.iter().sum();
        self.window.push_back(next_val);
        if self.window.len() > self.window_size {
            self.window.pop_front();
        }

        Some(next_val)
    }

    fn size_hint(&self) -> (usize, Option<usize>) {
        match self.max_count {
            Some(max) => {
                let remaining = max.saturating_sub(self.yielded);
                (remaining, Some(remaining))
            }
            None => (usize::MAX, None), // unbounded
        }
    }
}

fn main() {
    // Bounded Fibonacci: first 15 values
    let fibs: Vec<u128> = WindowFib::fibonacci(Some(15)).collect();
    println!("Fibonacci(15): {:?}", fibs);

    // Tribonacci with iterator adaptors
    let even_tribs: Vec<u128> = WindowFib::tribonacci(None)
        .take(20)
        .filter(|&x| x % 2 == 0)
        .collect();
    println!("Even tribonacci (from first 20): {:?}", even_tribs);

    // Custom 4-window (tetranacci) — map to strings, enumerate
    let labeled: Vec<String> = WindowFib::new(&[0, 0, 0, 1], None)
        .take(12)
        .enumerate()
        .map(|(i, val)| format!("T[{}]={}", i, val))
        .collect();
    println!("Tetranacci: {:?}", labeled);

    // Zip two iterators, sum pairs
    let pair_sums: Vec<u128> = WindowFib::fibonacci(None)
        .zip(WindowFib::tribonacci(None))
        .take(10)
        .map(|(a, b)| a + b)
        .collect();
    println!("Fib + Trib pair sums: {:?}", pair_sums);

    // Scan to get running sum of fibonacci
    let running_sum: Vec<u128> = WindowFib::fibonacci(None)
        .take(10)
        .scan(0u128, |state, x| {
            *state += x;
            Some(*state)
        })
        .collect();
    println!("Running sum of fib: {:?}", running_sum);
}
```

instruction: Create a Rust iterator adaptor that implements a 'windows' pattern over any iterator — yielding overlapping windows of size N as Vec<T> where T: Clone. Demonstrate how to implement it as an extension trait so it can be called on any iterator.
output:
<think>
Rust's standard library has `slice::windows()` but no equivalent for arbitrary iterators. To build one, I need a custom iterator struct that wraps an inner iterator and maintains a buffer of the last N items.

The design: an extension trait `IterWindows` with a method `sliding_windows(n)` that returns a `SlidingWindows<I>` struct. This struct holds the inner iterator, window size, a VecDeque buffer, and a done flag.

The buffer management: on the first call to next(), fill the buffer with N items from the inner iterator. Yield a clone of the buffer as a Vec. On subsequent calls, pull one more item, push it to the back, pop the front, and yield the new window. When the inner iterator is exhausted, return None.

T must be Clone because we're yielding windows that share elements — each item appears in up to N windows, so we must clone. Using VecDeque for O(1) push_back/pop_front.

The extension trait pattern: `trait IterWindows: Iterator + Sized` with a blanket impl for all iterators. The method consumes self (moves the iterator into the adaptor struct). This is the same pattern used by itertools.

Edge cases: window size 0 (panic or return empty), window size larger than the iterator (yield nothing), inner iterator exactly N items (yield one window).

I should also consider implementing size_hint() — if the inner iterator has an upper bound U, windows produces max(0, U - N + 1) items.
</think>

```rust
use std::collections::VecDeque;

struct SlidingWindows<I: Iterator> {
    inner: I,
    size: usize,
    buffer: VecDeque<I::Item>,
    initialized: bool,
    done: bool,
}

impl<I> Iterator for SlidingWindows<I>
where
    I: Iterator,
    I::Item: Clone,
{
    type Item = Vec<I::Item>;

    fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item> {
        if self.done {
            return None;
        }

        if !self.initialized {
            // Fill the initial window
            for _ in 0..self.size {
                match self.inner.next() {
                    Some(item) => self.buffer.push_back(item),
                    None => {
                        self.done = true;
                        return None; // not enough items for a single window
                    }
                }
            }
            self.initialized = true;
            return Some(self.buffer.iter().cloned().collect());
        }

        // Slide: remove front, add next from inner
        match self.inner.next() {
            Some(item) => {
                self.buffer.pop_front();
                self.buffer.push_back(item);
                Some(self.buffer.iter().cloned().collect())
            }
            None => {
                self.done = true;
                None
            }
        }
    }

    fn size_hint(&self) -> (usize, Option<usize>) {
        let (lower, upper) = self.inner.size_hint();
        if self.initialized {
            (lower, upper.map(|u| u + 1))
        } else {
            let lo = lower.saturating_sub(self.size).saturating_add(if lower >= self.size { 1 } else { 0 });
            let hi = upper.map(|u| if u >= self.size { u - self.size + 1 } else { 0 });
            (lo, hi)
        }
    }
}

// Extension trait — blanket impl for all iterators
trait IterWindows: Iterator + Sized {
    fn sliding_windows(self, size: usize) -> SlidingWindows<Self> {
        assert!(size > 0, "Window size must be at least 1");
        SlidingWindows {
            inner: self,
            size,
            buffer: VecDeque::with_capacity(size),
            initialized: false,
            done: false,
        }
    }
}

impl<I: Iterator + Sized> IterWindows for I {}

fn main() {
    // Basic sliding window over a range
    let windows: Vec<Vec<i32>> = (1..=7).sliding_windows(3).collect();
    println!("Windows of 3 over 1..=7:");
    for w in &windows {
        println!("  {:?}", w);
    }

    // Chain with other adaptors
    let max_per_window: Vec<i32> = (1..=10)
        .sliding_windows(4)
        .map(|w| *w.iter().max().unwrap())
        .collect();
    println!("Max per window: {:?}", max_per_window);

    // Moving average
    let moving_avg: Vec<f64> = vec![1.0, 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 2.0, 8.0, 4.0]
        .into_iter()
        .sliding_windows(3)
        .map(|w| w.iter().sum::<f64>() / w.len() as f64)
        .collect();
    println!("Moving average (window=3): {:?}", moving_avg);

    // Works with strings too
    let bigrams: Vec<Vec<char>> = "hello".chars().sliding_windows(2).collect();
    println!("Character bigrams: {:?}", bigrams);
}
```

instruction: Build a parallel iterator pipeline in Rust using rayon that processes a large dataset: parse CSV records, filter by criteria, transform fields, group by a key, and aggregate results. Show how rayon's par_iter integrates with the standard iterator ecosystem.
output:
<think>
Rayon's parallel iterators mirror Rust's standard Iterator trait but execute across multiple threads automatically. The key insight is that `.par_iter()` replaces `.iter()` and most adaptors (map, filter, fold, reduce) have parallel equivalents.

For CSV processing, I'll parse records into structs first (sequential — I/O bound), then switch to parallel for the CPU-bound filter/transform/aggregate pipeline. The bridge between sequential and parallel is `.par_iter()` on a collected Vec or `.into_par_iter()` for owned data.

Grouping in parallel is tricky — standard `group_by` requires sorted data. With rayon, the pattern is: use `.fold()` with thread-local HashMaps, then `.reduce()` to merge them. Each thread builds its own HashMap, then we merge all maps. This avoids lock contention.

Alternatively, I can use rayon's `.fold()` + `.reduce()` directly for aggregation if I know the group keys upfront. But for arbitrary grouping, the fold-with-hashmap-then-merge pattern is the standard approach.

I should show the full pipeline: parse -> par_iter -> filter -> map (transform) -> fold (group+aggregate) -> reduce (merge). Also demonstrate that rayon respects work-stealing: if one thread finishes its chunk early, it steals work from others.

For the demo, I'll create synthetic data rather than reading a real CSV, to keep it self-contained. The aggregation will compute sum and count per group for an average.
</think>

```rust
use rayon::prelude::*;
use std::collections::HashMap;

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
struct SalesRecord {
    region: String,
    product: String,
    amount: f64,
    quantity: u32,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Default)]
struct RegionAgg {
    total_revenue: f64,
    total_units: u64,
    record_count: u64,
}

impl RegionAgg {
    fn merge(mut self, other: RegionAgg) -> Self {
        self.total_revenue += other.total_revenue;
        self.total_units += other.total_units;
        self.record_count += other.record_count;
        self
    }

    fn avg_revenue(&self) -> f64 {
        if self.record_count == 0 { 0.0 } else { self.total_revenue / self.record_count as f64 }
    }
}

fn parse_csv_line(line: &str) -> Option<SalesRecord> {
    let parts: Vec<&str> = line.split(',').collect();
    if parts.len() != 4 { return None; }
    Some(SalesRecord {
        region: parts[0].trim().to_string(),
        product: parts[1].trim().to_string(),
        amount: parts[2].trim().parse().ok()?,
        quantity: parts[3].trim().parse().ok()?,
    })
}

fn main() {
    // Simulate CSV data (sequential parse — I/O bound)
    let raw_lines = vec![
        "North,Widget,150.00,10", "South,Gadget,200.00,5",
        "North,Gadget,175.50,8",  "East,Widget,90.00,20",
        "South,Widget,300.00,15", "East,Gadget,50.00,2",
        "North,Widget,220.00,12", "West,Gadget,180.00,7",
        "South,Widget,95.00,3",   "West,Widget,400.00,25",
        "East,Widget,130.00,9",   "North,Gadget,260.00,14",
    ];

    // Step 1: Sequential parse into Vec (I/O bound, not worth parallelizing)
    let records: Vec<SalesRecord> = raw_lines
        .iter()
        .filter_map(|line| parse_csv_line(line))
        .collect();

    println!("Parsed {} records", records.len());

    // Step 2: Parallel pipeline — filter, transform, group, aggregate
    let aggregated: HashMap<String, RegionAgg> = records
        .par_iter()
        // Filter: only records with amount > 100
        .filter(|r| r.amount > 100.0)
        // Transform: apply 10% tax adjustment
        .map(|r| SalesRecord {
            region: r.region.clone(),
            product: r.product.clone(),
            amount: r.amount * 0.90, // post-tax
            quantity: r.quantity,
        })
        // Fold: thread-local grouping into HashMaps
        .fold(
            HashMap::new,
            |mut acc: HashMap<String, RegionAgg>, record| {
                let entry = acc.entry(record.region.clone()).or_default();
                entry.total_revenue += record.amount;
                entry.total_units += record.quantity as u64;
                entry.record_count += 1;
                acc
            },
        )
        // Reduce: merge thread-local maps into one
        .reduce(
            HashMap::new,
            |mut a, b| {
                for (key, val) in b {
                    let entry = a.entry(key).or_default();
                    *entry = std::mem::take(entry).merge(val);
                }
                a
            },
        );

    // Results
    println!("\nRegion Aggregations (amount > 100, post-tax):");
    for (region, agg) in &aggregated {
        println!(
            "  {}: revenue={:.2}, units={}, count={}, avg={:.2}",
            region, agg.total_revenue, agg.total_units,
            agg.record_count, agg.avg_revenue()
        );
    }

    // Bonus: parallel find_first and sum
    let highest: Option<&SalesRecord> = records
        .par_iter()
        .max_by(|a, b| a.amount.partial_cmp(&b.amount).unwrap());
    println!("\nHighest sale: {:?}", highest);

    let total: f64 = records.par_iter().map(|r| r.amount).sum();
    println!("Total revenue (all records): {:.2}", total);
}
```

instruction: Implement an iterator that lazily evaluates a tree structure using depth-first and breadth-first strategies in Rust. The tree should be generic, and the iterators should work with standard iterator adaptors like take(), filter(), and collect().
output:
<think>
For tree traversal iterators, I need two strategies: DFS (using a stack) and BFS (using a queue). Both wrap around a generic tree structure and implement Iterator.

The tree structure: a generic `Tree<T>` with a value of type T and a Vec of children. This is a standard rose tree (n-ary tree).

For DFS: maintain a `Vec<&Tree<T>>` as a stack. Pop from the back, yield the value, push children in reverse order (so leftmost child is processed first). This gives pre-order traversal.

For BFS: maintain a `VecDeque<&Tree<T>>` as a queue. Pop from the front, yield the value, push all children to the back. This processes level by level.

Both iterators borrow the tree, so they need lifetime parameters: `DfsIter<'a, T>` and `BfsIter<'a, T>`. The Item type is `&'a T`.

I'll add methods on Tree<T>: `.dfs()` returns DfsIter, `.bfs()` returns BfsIter. Since both implement Iterator, all standard adaptors work automatically.

For a clean API, I'll also implement IntoIterator for &Tree<T> defaulting to DFS (the more common traversal). This lets you write `for node in &tree`.

Edge case: single node tree (no children) — both iterators yield one item and stop. Empty children vec is handled naturally since pushing nothing to the stack/queue means nothing more to process.
</think>

```rust
use std::collections::VecDeque;

#[derive(Debug)]
struct Tree<T> {
    value: T,
    children: Vec<Tree<T>>,
}

impl<T> Tree<T> {
    fn leaf(value: T) -> Self {
        Tree { value, children: vec![] }
    }

    fn branch(value: T, children: Vec<Tree<T>>) -> Self {
        Tree { value, children }
    }

    fn dfs(&self) -> DfsIter<'_, T> {
        DfsIter { stack: vec![self] }
    }

    fn bfs(&self) -> BfsIter<'_, T> {
        let mut queue = VecDeque::new();
        queue.push_back(self);
        BfsIter { queue }
    }
}

// Depth-First Iterator (pre-order)
struct DfsIter<'a, T> {
    stack: Vec<&'a Tree<T>>,
}

impl<'a, T> Iterator for DfsIter<'a, T> {
    type Item = &'a T;

    fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item> {
        let node = self.stack.pop()?;
        // Push children in reverse so leftmost is popped first
        for child in node.children.iter().rev() {
            self.stack.push(child);
        }
        Some(&node.value)
    }
}

// Breadth-First Iterator
struct BfsIter<'a, T> {
    queue: VecDeque<&'a Tree<T>>,
}

impl<'a, T> Iterator for BfsIter<'a, T> {
    type Item = &'a T;

    fn next(&mut self) -> Option<Self::Item> {
        let node = self.queue.pop_front()?;
        for child in &node.children {
            self.queue.push_back(child);
        }
        Some(&node.value)
    }
}

// Default iteration is DFS
impl<'a, T> IntoIterator for &'a Tree<T> {
    type Item = &'a T;
    type IntoIter = DfsIter<'a, T>;

    fn into_iter(self) -> Self::IntoIter {
        self.dfs()
    }
}

fn main() {
    //         1
    //       / | \
    //      2  3  4
    //     / \    |
    //    5   6   7
    //   /
    //  8
    let tree = Tree::branch(1, vec![
        Tree::branch(2, vec![
            Tree::branch(5, vec![Tree::leaf(8)]),
            Tree::leaf(6),
        ]),
        Tree::leaf(3),
        Tree::branch(4, vec![Tree::leaf(7)]),
    ]);

    // DFS traversal
    let dfs_order: Vec<&i32> = tree.dfs().collect();
    println!("DFS: {:?}", dfs_order); // [1, 2, 5, 8, 6, 3, 4, 7]

    // BFS traversal
    let bfs_order: Vec<&i32> = tree.bfs().collect();
    println!("BFS: {:?}", bfs_order); // [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8]

    // Composing with standard adaptors
    let first_4_dfs: Vec<&i32> = tree.dfs().take(4).collect();
    println!("First 4 DFS: {:?}", first_4_dfs);

    let even_bfs: Vec<&i32> = tree.bfs().filter(|&&v| v % 2 == 0).collect();
    println!("Even values (BFS): {:?}", even_bfs);

    let sum: i32 = tree.dfs().copied().sum();
    println!("Sum of all nodes: {}", sum);

    // Default iteration (DFS via IntoIterator)
    let via_for: Vec<i32> = (&tree).into_iter().copied().collect();
    println!("Default iteration: {:?}", via_for);

    // Find first value > 5
    let found = tree.bfs().find(|&&v| v > 5);
    println!("First BFS value > 5: {:?}", found);
}
```
